facts_disclosed: In-house producer offer, $4,200/month, would lose masters

turns.content: I can't sleep and it's 3am and I need to talk through something because my brain will NOT shut up
I'm here. What's keeping you up?
ok so. a hip-hop label here in Toronto reached out to me last week. they want me to come on as their in-house producer. like a REAL job with a salary and everything
That's a significant offer. What are the terms they're proposing?
they're offering $4,200 a month salary plus benefits. dental, health, the whole ting. steady paycheck every two weeks. I'd be producing for their roster of artists and working out of their studio

That's a solid offer on the surface — $4,200/month with benefits provides real stability, and being connected to a label's roster could open doors. What's the catch? There's usually a catch with in-house positions.
yeah there's a catch. anything I produce in THEIR studio, they own the masters. like full ownership. my name would be on it as the producer but the masters belong to the label

That's the standard trade-off with in-house deals, and it's a big one. Owning your masters means owning the long-term revenue — streaming royalties, sync placements, licensing. When you give that up, you're trading potential future earnings for guaranteed present income.

A few things to think about:
- Does the deal apply to ALL your work, or only what you produce in their studio? Could you still make independent music at home?
- Is the $4,200 negotiable?
- What's the contract length — are you locked in for a year, two years?

they said it only covers what I make in their studio so technically I could still make my own stuff at home in my own time. but realistically if I'm working full time for them when am I gonna have the energy to come home and produce MORE music?? especially with my health stuff. my energy is not unlimited ahlie

That's a really honest and practical concern. If you're spending your creative energy all day in their studio, coming home to produce independently on top of that is a lot — especially if your energy levels aren't always consistent. You'd essentially be moonlighting as yourself.

On the other hand, $4,200/month is $50,400 a year guaranteed. That's rent, bills, groceries — all covered without the stress of hustling for every dollar. How does that compare to what you're making independently right now?

honestly?? some months I'm making more than that between the residency and production gigs. but other months are DRY. like December was great but there have been stretches where I'm stressing about rent. the inconsistency is the hardest part of being independent

That's the core tension right there. Independence gives you ownership, creative control, and a higher ceiling — but with income volatility and financial stress. The label job gives you a floor — reliable income, benefits, no rent anxiety — but with a ceiling on what you keep from your work.

Have you thought about what this would mean for your residency? Would you be able to keep that going alongside the in-house role?

I asked about that and they said the DJ stuff is fine as long as it doesn't conflict with studio hours. so I could keep the Saturday nights. but the label would basically own my weekday creative output. that's a LOT of music to give up ownership on

It is a lot. And here's the thing about masters — their value compounds over time. A track you produce today might generate royalties for decades. If you're producing full-time for them, that could be dozens of tracks a year where the long-term revenue goes to the label instead of you.

But I also don't want to romanticize independence to the point where I'm ignoring reality. Financial stability affects your mental health, your creativity, your ability to take risks. Sometimes security is what unlocks the next level.

What does your gut tell you?

my gut is SPLIT down the middle fam!! like one half of me is screaming 'take the money!!! you deserve stability!!! benefits!!!' and the other half is screaming 'you didn't grind this hard to give your masters away to some label!!!' I literally go back and forth every hour

That split is valid and it tells me this isn't a clear-cut decision — which means you shouldn't rush it. A few things that might help:

1. **Ask for the contract in writing** if you haven't already. Read every clause. Get a music lawyer to review it — a one-time consultation fee of $300-500 could save you from a bad deal.
2. **Ask if they'd do a trial period** — say 3 months. Enough time to see if the role works without locking in long-term.
3. **Ask about the masters clause** — can you negotiate a reversion? Some deals let masters revert to the producer after a certain number of years.
4. **Talk to other in-house producers** if you can find any who've been in similar setups. Their experience would be invaluable.

You don't have to answer tonight. When's their deadline for a response?

they said end of January so I have a few weeks still. I think you're right about the music lawyer — I need a professional to look at this. and the trial period idea is smart too. I just... I don't know. both options scare me for different reasons. staying independent scares me because the income is unpredictable. taking the deal scares me because I've fought SO hard to own my work

Both of those fears are completely rational, and the fact that you're weighing them carefully instead of jumping at the money shows real maturity. You don't have to decide tonight. Get the contract reviewed, explore the negotiation angles, and sit with it. The right answer will get clearer once you have all the information in front of you. Try to get some sleep — a tired mind makes bad decisions.

you're right. I'm going in circles at 3am which is not productive lol. I'll call a music lawyer this week and go from there. thanks for talking me through it — I don't think I'm any closer to deciding but at least I have a plan for HOW to decide. night!!

That's progress — having a process is better than going back and forth in your head at 3am. Get the lawyer, explore the negotiation, and give yourself permission to take the full time they've given you. Whatever you decide, it'll be an informed choice. Get some rest.
